source.title: Freud Doctor Of The Human Soul

additional.context:
## Dieses Dokument

- Titel: Freud Doctor Of The Human Soul
- Signatur: SZ-AAP/W-AA58.7
- Datum: May 3. 1936.
- Sprache: Englisch
- Objekttyp: Zeitungsausschnitt
- Umfang: 1 Blatt

pages.text:
Sunday Dispatch, MAY 3, 1936. 3

Hope For Famous Windjammer

THERE is talk in Salcombe, Devon, of a fund to salve and refit the stranded Herzogin Cecilie, which is being slowly pounded to pieces on the rocks off Hope Cove.
Capt. Erickson and his young wife are supervising the salvage of the barque's cargo of grain.
Here is a picture of the salvage work yesterday.
Mrs. Erickson suffered another blow during the week. Her pet Alsatian dog was taken off to quarantine.

Boy Who Wanted To Go To The Opera
AND WROTE TO SIR THOMAS BEECHAM ABOUT IT

SIR Thomas Beecham has made 11-years-old Leonard Gunton the happiest boy in London by obtaining for him two seats for the May 15 performance of "Aida" at the Royal Opera House, Covent Garden.
Although only 11, Leonard loves opera, and after hearing the "Meistersinger" on the wireless, he asked his father to take him to Covent Garden.
He felt Mr. Samuel Gunton, a motor mechanic, was not up at five in the morning and went to the Royal Opera House to book his own queue.
He returned home disappointed. He wrote to Leonard, unknown to his parents, sat down and wrote this letter to Sir Thomas Beecham:
"Dear Sir Thomas Beecham,
"My teacher has been reading to us about 'Aida', which must be a wonderful opera.
"My Dad got up to Covent Garden Opera House at 5.45 this morning to try to get a seat for me for to-night, but it was hopeless, and terribly disappointing. I've plucked up courage enough to write to you wondering if you could help me get a seat for May 15 or 15, when you are again producing 'Aida'."
A Sunday Dispatch reporter was able to tell Leonard last night that Sir Thomas is sending him two tickets for the May 15 performance.
Leonard inherits his taste for music from his father, who was in the band of the 2nd Dragoon Guards and was a State Trumpeter at the Durbar when the late King-Emperor visited India.

LABOUR DAY IN HYDE PARK
NON-STOP TALK FROM SIX PLATFORMS

LONDON Socialists will celebrate May Day with a mass demonstration in Hyde Park this afternoon.
Mr. C. R. Attlee, leader of the Opposition, heads the list of speakers. He will be supported by 11 other M.P.s, Socialist members of the London County Council and trade union and co-operative society officials.
Marchers will assemble in 30 different districts.
The organisers estimate that 60,000 people will attend.

45 YEARS' SERVICE
After 45 years with the G.W.R., Mr. H. J. Snook, parcels agent at Paddington, has retired.
He was responsible for the despatch of newspapers from Paddington.
Mr. Percy Cambridge, senior relief stationmaster of the London division, succeeds him.

WARNS OF DISASTER IF PEACE IS NOT STRONG
ARMS—NOT PACTS—PEACE
The Writing Is On The Wall

WE DO NOT WANT TO MUDDLE THROUGH ANOTHER WAR AFTER FEARFUL LOSSES. WE MUST BE STRONG ENOUGH TO MAKE IT TOO RISKY FOR ANYONE TO START A WAR WITHOUT PAYING DUE ATTENTION TO OUR VIEWS ON THE SUBJECT.

This statement was made by General Sir Walter Kirke, Director-General of the Territorial Army, at Shrewsbury yesterday.
"There is a grave risk," he said. "The writing is on the wall."
Lord Salisbury's words in 1912 were terribly true to-day. He said:
There is one way in which Britain can have peace, not only with Germany, but with every other power, and that is to present such a battle-front that no Power—or probable combination of Powers, shall dare to attack her without the certainty of disaster.
Sir Walter said the trend of events was towards an area and where those gamblers might take the supreme risk of a world war, if by so doing there were a reasonable chance of improving the position of their own country at the expense of someone else's.

DANGER OF WEAKNESS
Some critics have believed that "might is right," and that "Heaven helps those who help themselves." China and Abyssinia, being not, perhaps, because they were too strong, but because they were too weak. Surely, for us, there was a lesson here.
It was unwise to play any decisive rôle in the duty of preserving the peace at the world war level, be strong.
We must have an army commensurate with our responsibilities. Though mechanically best entirely changed, though the Navy was no longer paramount, though distances had shrunk and with them our sea protection, we still pinned our faith to the voluntary system, which had been abandoned by practically everyone else.

GRAVE RISK
This was a grave risk. If it did not produce the men we required it was not a risk but a certainty of disaster.
The situation of the Territorial Army to-day was most disgusting. In 1914 its strength was 272,000. To-day it was less than half that figure.
Many people were misled by the idea that in some occult manner the resolutions of the League of Nations were a substitute for any practical ability to put them into effect. Mussolini should surely have exploded that idea, but he did not seem to have done so thoroughly in that respect as he had been in Abyssinia.
"There must surely be some members of the League of Nations Union understanding that good resolutions without the necessary force behind them are useless, and they should educate those of less intelligence," he went on.
"The clergy, in some cases, do not give the support which we have the right to expect. Let them remember that while in Rome one was a purely pacifist religion, it has been swept out of India by more militant creeds.
"Let them not forget that we in England, at the end of the day, solely because many centuries ago the knights of old were overthrown, took the Mongol hordes by force of arms.

BULWARK OF CHRISTIANITY
"England, indeed, is the great bulwark of Christianity in Europe, and in defending it, those who defend we are also defending our religion.
"The ethics of Christianity have not changed. Those who believe in the soldier to-day take upon themselves a highly responsible, if not painful, tion, they also condemn the millions of world-wide adherents. Quite and clear for their Fatherland, their families, and their faith."
The women could take a hand. There was no old saying that "the hand which rocks the cradle rules the world," and he would suggest to them with asking them to give a friendly push to the Territorial Army works.

DUTY TO JOIN UP
Lord Luke, speaking at the opening of a new drill hall for Territorials at Biggleswade, Beds., at the weekend, said:
"There is nothing more likely to keep us out of war in the future than to impress on the Continent that we are prepared to defend ourselves and our great possessions, and we can put our country in a fairly secure position if we all do our duty under the voluntary system."
He referred to the Territorial Army, "on which we depend to a large extent for the safety of our homes because the Army may be required overseas," and said it was the duty of every healthy and able-bodied man to try to join it.

ARMLESS MAN'S 7ft. 6in. BRIDE
An armless man to be married at Ramsgate this month intends to place the ring on his bride's finger and sign the register with his feet. His bride is 7ft. 6in. tall.
A girl of 18, weighing 40st., will act as bridesmaid.

PEER RECONCILED TO ACTRESS WIFE
EARL and Countess Poulett have decided to re-establish a home together, according to a public announcement made yesterday.
The announcement said:
"Notice is hereby given that the Earl and Countess Poulett, having re-established a home together, Earl Poulett is prepared to accept his legal obligations to the same extent as previous to March 31, 1936."
A previous notice made public on April 2 stated that the Earl withdrew all authority for his wife to pledge his credit and that he would not be responsible for her debts.
Lord Poulett married Miss Ross last June at St. Paul's Church, Knightsbridge. He is 26 and she is 28.
Miss Ross made a name on the stage as Catherine of Russia in "Casanova" at the Coliseum. She also sat as a model for Mr. Epstein.

'THE BERGNER' IS BETTER TO RESUME BARRIE'S REHEARSALS
MISS Elizabeth Bergner, when some screen and stage star, is making a rapid recovery from her operation for appendicitis.
She made her first appearance since her illness with her husband, Mr. Paul Czinner, and Mr. C. B. Cochran, on the Royal Opera House, Covent Garden, on Friday night.
"She is feeling much better and is looking forward to being able to start rehearsals for 'The Boy David' in the early autumn," Mr. Cochran said to the Sunday Dispatch yesterday.
The production of Sir James Barrie's new play, "The Boy David," had to be postponed owing to Miss Bergner's illness.
Mr. Cochran is delighted with the success of his Adelphi experiment and in the 19 days since he began showing his "Follow the Sun" twice nightly, no fewer than 37,000 people have paid for admission.
These figures are a record.
"The twice-nightly show at cheap prices has proved itself an astonishing success," Mr. Cochran said, "in spite of the fact that the show cost me £25,000 to produce, and the running expenses are £3,000 a week.
"The cheaper prices and the more convenient time for many people are meeting a popular demand."

Peckham Zero Hour
POLLING day in Peckham by-election is drawing nearer, and interest among politicians is growing intense.
Captain P. G. Harvey, Conservative candidate, fresh to the division, has to fight a man who has contested and canvassed the division before, and who knows the people.
But dapper, good-looking Captain Harvey's popularity is growing.
In his final detailed appeal to the electors Captain Harvey is emphasising the work of the National Government during the last four years, but his supporters feel that if he emphasised more the precautions which he should be taken to protect London from air raids his position would be solidified.
pages.notes: Zeitungsseite, gedruckter Text. Mehrspaltiges Layout. Enthält verschiedene Artikel und Anzeigen.

pages.text:
4 Sunday Dispatch, MAY 3, 1936.

FREUD
Doctor Of The
Human Soul
By STEFAN ZWEIG

* If our generation thinks of, speaks of, many things more freely than its predecessors did . . . Sigmund Freud's perceptions have largely contributed to this.
* Every one of us has been influenced by him.

SIGMUND Freud, whose discoveries have revolutionized psychology, will be 80 on Wednesday.
Here is a searching study of the personality and work of this remarkable man by Stefan Zweig, the famous Austrian author.

STEFAN ZWEIG—
Born in Vienna in 1881; took the degree of Ph.D. at the University of his native town.
He soon devoted himself entirely to literature.
A number of poems—highly prized to-day—were followed by such novels as "Amok," "Burning Secret," "Fear," and others, since turned into highly successful plays and motion pictures.
Zweig is widely travelled and has visited all continents. His essays and biographical works, like his novels, are international best sellers.
"Fouché," "Mental Healers," "Marie Antoinette," "Mary Stuart," have reached giant circulations in England and America.
Most of his works have been translated into practically every civilised tongue, including Chinese and Japanese.

IN each minute of our world millions and milliards of thoughts are thought.
All these millions and milliards of thoughts are extinguished and vanish in the course of this minute; the very next knows them no more.
But among these millions and milliards of transitory, evanescent, un-fruitful and valueless thoughts there is sometimes generated a special, active, fruitful thought which is not extinguished, but sweeps on, awakes other thoughts, seizes them and carries them with it—an invention, a discovery, a perception that changes the world.
Such thoughts, such productive thoughts are immeasurably rare; few are conceived in the course of each decade. But on them depends the changing of our spiritual, our civilised, our actual world.
Sigmund Freud, whose eightieth birthday we are celebrating, has in the long and active span of his life, thought several such thoughts.
He has, furthermore, thought them to their conclusion, and he has in logical sequence bound them into a science.
The effective force of these thoughts upon our world has been incomparable. They have completely changed our previous conceptions not only in the original sphere of medicine and psychology, but the motive force of the psycho-analytical method of inquiry that he discovered was so powerful that it gradually affected all forms of mental activity.
He has decisively influenced not only contemporary poetry, and literature, teaching, and literary research, but also the very formal of our human relationships.

AS I write, I feel that some will contest this assertion. "Exaggeration!" I hear someone say. "I know that I cannot possibly have been influenced by him. I have never read Freud."
And says another: "I have no intention of being influenced by all his theories. I know his ideas. BUT I won't, have anything to do with them."
But how erroneous to think that an idea has only an immediate effect, and that one must read the original work of a thinker to be influenced by him.
So the Individual cannot claim to be uninfluenced by Freud, even though he has never held a book of his in his hand.

NEW!
For almost every significant novel of the last twenty years one can be read, and many a play that he has been influenced by him in a way of science.
Many words even to-day circulate in the blood of the language, and the old, pre-Freudia psychology is in use, derive from his terminology.
At school, either one comes to the doctor's consulting room, many of his discoveries have long been put into practice, because they have slowly been the common property—perception of an individual to be the perception of our period.

THESE special ideas and discoveries of Freud—what they are, what they were? Abbreviations are always inexact and unjust.
But if we are to abbreviate, we would say that Freud's most important achievement is that he gave us the key to the explanation of the nature and the inner mechanism of the human soul, and the same began with him a completely new kind of therapy, that is, at same time, a new method of treating the ailments of the soul.

REPRESSION
IT is as sharp as the cut of a knife, the line of demarcation that since the beginning of the twentieth century divides the old psychology, the pre-Freudian psychology, and the new.
The old, the pre-Freudia psychology, believed in the supremacy of the brain over the blood.
Indeed, it knew—especially those people whose professions forced them to be acquainted with what is dangerous in man, doctors, judges, confessors, teachers—how many secrets lie concealed behind the external staid decency of civilised people.
But the old psychology and pedagogy held that it is better to simply dominate his dangerous instincts by will-power and reason, and adjust them thoroughly to the social requirements.
It demanded of people that they should not be conscious of these instincts; think as little as possible of them, never speak of them, repress them, keep silent about everything that is unwelcome, was the teaching of the old psychology.
But Freud asked rightly: "Where are these instincts being pushed?"
And he replied: "From the consciousness down into the unconscious, and there they become even more dangerous than in their conscious form."
That is why he demanded un-covering, instead of covering up, and recognising the inner dangers, instead of ignoring them.
Just as a bad tooth is not cured by merely desdening the pain, in stead of attacking the root of the trouble, so ailments of the soul are not healed by ignoring them or concealing them, but by investigating their origins, by penetrating to the roots of the trouble.

BEFORE Freud—the text books on nervous disorders up to 1900 already seem to us absurdly suggestive in the focus of Mongolian and Tungoosian witch doctors—the psychopathic were treated by stimulating their skin with electricity, or by trying to quieten their nerves.
Freud was the first to treat disorders of the soul not through the skin, but through the soul; he was the first to recognise that the soul has its own laws, demands its own, a different mands its own, a different method from that of the material body.

HATRED
I still remember very distinctly when 1-a boy in Vienna-first heard of Freud. A friend of our family, who was very hysterical, and like most hysterical people loved to consult and worry doctor after doctor, had been to see Freud, and had come home in a state of wild excitement.
After much effort, we managed to find out what had happened to her: the doctor had, in diagnosing, questioned her about matters that she would not confess even to herself.
She simply shook with hatred, with indignation whenever his name was mentioned.

SHE was not alone. The young nerve specialist, when he in his practice of the cold water cure and started his psycho-analytical methods, lost at one stroke all his patients; while the University, the Medical Faculty, was indignant with a doctor who seri-ously investigated such old-wives' tales as dreams, and who dared openly to emphasise the influence of sex upon many disturbances of the soul.
It was pained for a long time by the thought that such an audacious innovator, called to the faculty's professor of the Faculty, and if Sigmund Freud still has not received the Nobel Prize it is largely to be traced back to this opposition.

COURAGE
Freud—and this is evidence of his moral greatness—has never allowed himself to be diverted by opposition. In addition to his psychological genius, he has also the human courage to think his thoughts to their conclusion, and to express them in spite of all the opposition of all the Faculties.
He went further and further, he dared more and more.
Though scoffed at through the untrue exaggeration that he sought to see sex in everything and everywhere by the sexual, he demonstrated how deeply instincts reach in their unconscious forms back to childhood, and how late disturbances often have their origin in the earliest years.
No resistance could ever hinder him from carrying out his plan; no moral indignation moved him to speak more gently and circumspectly about things which he considered it his scientific duty to make plain.

HE has been equally inflexible in respect of the other danger that confronts every mental worker: the danger of being seduced by fame.
For suddenly fame had come, and in its most perilous form: after the war psycho-analysis became fashionable, and the public began to make free with it in a way which must have been painful to so severe and scientific an investigator as Freud.

FAME
Dilettantes wrote diluted rehashes, It was chattered about in every drawing-room, dreams were interpreted in the way in which people solve crossword puzzles.
Freud was in request for lectures, for introductions, for films, he was invited to all countries and cities: after three decades of solitary achievement he had become the most celebrated savant of his generation.
But just as disapproval could not break him, the tumult of fame could not lead him astray; the scholar has remained silent and persistent with his work, he has gone his way erect and determined, and always only his own way.

THIS splendid determination not to deviate from his path makes Sigmund Freud a great moral figure, a model to every artist, to every scholar.
Work is his life, and nothing but work. For decades he has lived in the same house in the same Viennese suburb, in complete retirement.
He has never been seen at social gatherings or festivities. But within these narrow walls he experiences the world.
In his small room, decorated with little Egyptian and Greek plastics—Freud loves these things for their symbolism—more confessions have been heard than in the confessional of Notre Dame; for years Freud has each day devoted eight or ten hours to psycho-analysis, and it would be hard to demonstrate what an exhausting effort this denotes.

WORK
For, with each treatment, which lasts for weeks and months, each individual is gradually reminded and made conscious of his whole soul past, until the point of the actual disturbance is revealed.
This demands of the doctor, in addition to an unusual degree of tact, also an exceptional feat of memory and the power of questioning.
I have often thought, and I have said so to Freud, that if all these years there had been a shorthand-typist kept in the next room, or a concealed microphone, posterity would find itself in possession of a unique, incomparable document of life experiences, that now is known to him alone.

His scientific and theoretical works were written mostly at night, after his exhausting day's work; one may without exaggeration say of this most tireless of scholars that save in sleep his brain has never rested, and to-day, in his eightieth year, the intensity of his universal interest is still entirely undiminished.
He follows all manifestations in the cultural world, he reads every important book, and is in correspondence—he still writes every letter in his own hand—with all essential research workers. One can think of no more concentrated man than he is, and hardly of anyone from whom so much peace and calm emanates.

FULFILMENT
The clear, strong, dark eye of this old man goes deep, and I believe that no one has ever dared to lie in his presence, because they must have felt his keen penetrating glance, and because people know, too, that this man, with his wonderful humanity, also understands kindly and helpfully what is most mysterious in a man's life.
It is impossible not to be sincere in front of this man, who increases the sincerity which exists in our world, and has made it everyone's task to know himself more profoundly.
What satisfaction, then, to know that this man in his eightieth year is still at work with unbroken mental faculty and with the same moral task to know himself more and thus increase the truth in our world, and who at the same time has shown us in exemplary fashion in his own life how against all opposition one carries such an elevated task to fulfilment.
[WORLD COPYRIGHT RESERVED]

SIGMUND FREUD—
Born Moravia. Studied in Vienna most of his life. Founded psycho-analysis.
Freud discovered that many neurotic troubles were due to conflicts in the mind—the conscious, socially trained, and the suppressed primitive tendencies of the unconscious.
pages.notes: Zeitungsseite, gedruckter Text. Artikel über Sigmund Freud von Stefan Zweig. Enthält Anzeigen (Spratt's Bonio, Keating's, Vitadatio, Lux, Ovaltine).

confidence_notes: Der Text ist sehr gut lesbar, da es sich um einen hochwertigen Zeitungsdruck handelt. Die wenigen Überlagerungen durch Archivierungshilfen (Farbskala/Grauskala) auf Seite 3 beeinträchtigen die Lesbarkeit des darunterliegenden Textes auf Seite 2 nur minimal.
